text:
Track cycling has finally got underway at the Paris 2024Olympics. The public will be eagerly tuning in to watch Great Britain thrive in one of its most successful and entertaining sports, though they can be forgiven for struggling to get their heads around its variety of formats.

Team GB has built a golden generation of velodrome stars since the turn of the 21st century, establishing themselves as the dominant force in track cycling. They really began to impose themselves on the world stage at the Beijing 2008 Olympics, since which Great Britain has clinched 22 gold medals across the last four Summer Games.

Olympic track cycling is split into three sprint events (individual sprint, team sprint and keirin) and three endurance events (Madison, omnium and team pursuit), most of which were only introduced to the programme at London 2012. It can be somewhat of a challenge for beginners to distinguish between the disciplines and understand how the rules differ for each - soExpress Sportwill look to explain the key details below to help you stay on top of every pedal.

In the keirin, 30 cyclists will race over six laps with a motorised bike setting the pace for the first half of the race. After that, they can go all out for the remaining three laps.

However, riders can face disqualification if they overtake the pace-setter bike before it leaves the track. In the event of a photo finish where competitors cannot be separated, both advance (or share medals in the case of a final).

Competitors are divided into five heats of six riders, with the top two from each heat automatically progressing to the quarter-finals. Twelve reach the semi-finals and an elite six battle it out in the medal final.

Those who don't make the cut in the first round have a second chance in the repechage round, where the top two finishers from four heats of five riders can keep their medal dreams alive.



The omnium event in track cycling is slightly more complex as teams accumulate points across four different formats: the scratch race, tempo race, elimination race and the points race.

In the scratch, tempo and elimination races, riders earn 40 points for coming first, 38 points for second place, 36 points for third, and so on. Men are required to complete 40 laps while women must complete 30; however, this increases to 100 laps for men and 80 laps for women in the points race.

The points race also has a different points system, awarding five points for first place, three points for second, two points for third and one point for fourth. The final sprint offers double points - and it’s important to note that any rider who laps the field earns an extra 20 points, while being lapped results in a 20-point penalty.



The Madison is a two-person relay that can get very confusing. One rider actively competes while their partner remains inactive at the top of the track.

Team-mates alternate between racing and resting, with points awarded in intermediate sprints every 10 laps. The men's Madison spans 200 laps (around 31 miles) while the women's race covers only 120 laps (roughly 18.5 miles), with teams ranked in medal positions based on points accumulated.

Each sprint rewards five points for first place, three points for second, two points for third and one point for fourth. Any team that laps a competitor earns an additional 20 points, while 20 points are deducted for any team lapped. In the final sprint, double points are awarded.



In the individual sprint, a qualification round involving 30 riders will see participants complete three laps of the track, though only the last 200 metres are timed. The 24 fastest competitors then advance to the elimination rounds, where seeded opponents are gradually whittled down to the last 16, then 12, then the quarter-finals, semis and eventually the gold medal final.

Each of the first three elimination rounds (before the quarters) comprises a repechage round, which allows a set number of riders a second chance to advance if they win their heat. Four extra places are on offer in each of the first two elimination rounds, while just two advance from the third.

In the team sprint event, both men's and women's categories field eight teams each, with three riders per team. However, only the final lap of the third rider is counted once they've got up to speed.

The qualification round is a time trial before teams are seeded into head-to-head matchups for round 1 (starting at opposite sides of the track). From there, the two fastest winners advance to the gold medal final, while the two slowest winners compete in the bronze medal final.

The repechage system is used in numerous Olympic events, including rowing. It is perhaps most recognised in the track cycling events at the Games.

The repechage offers a second chance to those who have been defeated in a specific round. It is used in the first round of the Keirin and the elimination round of the Individual Sprint, as outlined above.

title: Olympics 2024: Keirin, omnium, madison & repechage explained | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Tom Sunderland, Kasra Moradi
description: The track cycling is well underway at the Tokyo 2020 Olympics, but fans may be struggling to keep up with the different formats that make up the thrilling sport
